title: Uhhh…ummm….struggling to think of anything. The vending machine?
pros: Uhhh…ummm….struggling to think of anything. The vending machine?
cons:
My time at KPMG Global Services is proving to be the worst experience of my life. Every day I break down crying and feel like I’m a fifth class fellow being bullied again. I feel compelled to warn others before they make the mistake of joining. Here are some points: 
1. No structure, no balance: There are no defined working hours. Enjoy. Logging off at a decent time will forever be a dream. If you even think about it, you’ll be mocked and ridiculed. Your work ethic will be questioned regardless of what your work quality is like. Respect for personal time simply doesn’t exist. In fact you’ll be constantly reminded to take ownership of the very crucial and critical work that’s going on. Yes because it’s life-saving, cancer-curing work…skip lunch! 
2. Next-level micromanagement: Each minute and each hour of what you’re doing will be monitored and schooled by someone who’s senior to you. The micromanagement is suffocating to the point where you want to rip your hair out. 
3. No respect for well-being: Forget about empathy here. Get ready to hear “if you’re sick then you should have told us, now our work is all messed up because of you.” Yes, because people plan in advance about falling sick, right? And because your work is somehow fully dependent on just one low-level employee? There have been times when people on sick leave have been called into meetings. I mentioned lack of empathy right…yes. People here are like robots. They don’t respond to any human condition. They don’t seem to have souls. Their programming is just one thing: work work work. Be fully utilised. Stay busy. Keep the team stats high. Be visible. People have become robotic and rude after staying in the system for years. 
4. Hostile and uncomfortable work environment: Seniors will cross boundaries. Especially if you’re female. You’re going to have at least some senior who will make you uncomfortable for sure. I have noticed this with my female colleagues. Poor girls. Also, the culture is full of uncomfortable vibes, mockery, unnecessary jabs, and ridiculous jokes. If you are a junior, get ready for each and every single senior person to come and exercise intimidation and forced “tough love” onto you. Your personal passions and interests will be crushed, I am not kidding. People will make fun of you for not having the dream of chasing money. 
5. Fake promises and bait & switch: The best of the worst. Your job won’t match the JD and interview promises at all. You’ll stay tagged under one team and profile but be dumped with irrelevant, low-quality work. Any aspirations or ambition to specialise in any field will be crushed. 
6. No real collaboration: While they pretend to be helpful, the reality is backstabbing, gossip, and snitching. Asking for support or direction is a red flag apparently. 
7. Forced culture: There is a culture to be “visible” and it’s so forced. You’ll be pressured to attend pointless social gatherings with people you’ve already spent 12 hours around. Yeah, your wife and kid at home aren’t waiting to see you at all. 
If you value your time, your mental health, your career trajectory, and don’t want to cry every day, avoid this place. I wish I could name my specific team…because avoid this team like anything.

pros:
- Nice office, accessible area
- Great people across most levels - a number of directors who can really impart skills from both consulting and non-consulting backgrounds, and a lot of juniors with plenty of enthusiasm and willingness to learn. Generally quite friendly and collaborative and most people are pretty approachable. Clients generally also quite good.
- WFH and a few other initiatives like BSF and wellness efforts (sports, $1 bowls, fruits at the pantry), also have some pretty interesting employee recreation groups
- Plenty of opportunities to learn especially for tech, gaining industry exposure/expertise
- Relatively decent work-life balance depending on your project, at least in comparison to other Big4 (from what I hear)
cons:
- Salary and increments. Stingy management.
- Contradictory culture and practices. Want to be innovative but so old school in how they think, especially around performance. Very vague expectations around performance and promotions hence easy for them to reference vague criteria to not promote someone. Want to be "flat" in structure and for you to "take charge" but honestly super hierarchical and micromanaging. Want to hire the best talent but can't keep them cause they're not rewarded properly. Want to get people to speak up but when they say something different/contradicting, they are shut down or labeled.
- So very political. A lot of good people end up leaving cause of this. Favoritism and gossip culture is rampant. You can clearly see who is "in" and who isn't. Throwing people under the bus is normal. 
- Don't really have the right talent for advisory engagements tbh.

title: Active during the initial interview.
pros: Active during the initial interview.
cons:
I recently applied for a position at KPMG Philippines and was told to wait for two weeks for an update. I followed up before the two-week mark and was simply told to continue waiting. After several more days without any communication, I followed up again—only to be informed that I was not selected for the role.

What I found disappointing was the lack of proactive communication. I believe applicants deserve timely updates, especially after waiting as instructed. It’s disheartening to have to chase for answers, only to receive a rejection with no prior notice.

This experience left me with a negative impression of the company's recruitment process. Clear and respectful communication goes a long way, and unfortunately, that was lacking in this case.

pros:
Exposure to a wide range of internal legal advisory matters typical of a Big 4 setup.

Opportunity to handle commercial legal queries and grow your technical knowledge.

Platform to work with large internal clients and complex contracts.

Brand name of KPMG can be helpful on your résumé.
cons:
Toxic team environment marked by micromanagement and fear-based leadership.

Feedback is vague, inconsistent, and often delayed until year-end reviews, used more as a punitive tool than a developmental one.

Constructive suggestions or dissenting views are met with silent retaliation and targeted scrutiny.

Lack of psychological safety, raising concerns results in systemic backlash.

Team culture discourages collaboration; juniors are instructed not to seek help outside their assigned reviewer.

Marking system for legal advice mimics school-like grading and is demoralizing.

No recognition of small wins; only mistakes are amplified.

Confidence-eroding environment that undermines employee morale and wellbeing.

Perception of favoritism in performance assessments and career progression decisions.

Concerns over performance improvement plans being misused as a tool to phase out outspoken team members.
